sections.title: The Perfekt Tense
sections.explanation: In spoken German and everyday writing, the main past tense is the Perfekt (present perfect). It is formed with an auxiliary verb (haben or sein) + the past participle (Partizip II). The auxiliary verb takes position 2 in the sentence, and the past participle goes to the END of the clause. Structure: Subject + haben/sein (conjugated) + ... + Partizip II. Example: 'Ich habe Kaffee getrunken.' (I drank / have drunk coffee.) 'Wir sind nach Berlin gefahren.' (We drove / have driven to Berlin.)
sections.tip: Perfekt formula: auxiliary (haben or sein) in position 2 + Partizip II at the END. The participle always goes last!

exercises.prompt: Where does the past participle (Partizip II) go in a Perfekt sentence?
options: At the end of the clause
exercises.explanation: In the Perfekt, the auxiliary (haben/sein) takes position 2, and the past participle goes to the end: 'Ich habe Kaffee getrunken.' — 'getrunken' is at the end.

exercises.prompt: Which auxiliary verbs are used to form the Perfekt?
exercises.explanation: The Perfekt is formed with 'haben' (to have) or 'sein' (to be) + past participle. Most verbs use 'haben'; verbs of motion and state change typically use 'sein'.

exercises.prompt: Ich ___ heute viel gelernt. (I learned a lot today.) — auxiliary for lernen
exercises.explanation: 'lernen' uses 'haben': Ich habe heute viel gelernt. (I learned a lot today.)

exercises.prompt: What is the correct Perfekt of 'Ich trinke Kaffee'?
exercises.explanation: 'trinken' uses 'haben' (it's a transitive verb). The participle is 'getrunken'. Auxiliary in position 2, participle at end.

sections.title: Forming the Past Participle
sections.explanation: The Partizip II is formed differently for regular (weak) and irregular (strong) verbs. Regular verbs: ge- + stem + -(e)t. Example: machen → gemacht, spielen → gespielt, arbeiten → gearbeitet. Irregular verbs: ge- + changed stem + -en. These must be memorized. Examples: trinken → getrunken, fahren → gefahren, schreiben → geschrieben, gehen → gegangen, sprechen → gesprochen. Verbs with inseparable prefixes (be-, er-, ver-, ge-, ent-, zer-) do NOT add 'ge-': besuchen → besucht (not 'gebesucht'). Verbs ending in -ieren also do not add 'ge-': studieren → studiert.
sections.tip: Regular pattern: ge- + stem + -t (gemacht, gelernt). Irregular: ge- + new stem + -en (getrunken, gefahren). No 'ge-' for verbs with be-/ver-/er- etc.

exercises.prompt: What is the Partizip II of 'machen' (to make)?
exercises.explanation: Regular verb: ge- + mach- + t = gemacht. Er hat das gemacht = He did that.

exercises.prompt: What is the Partizip II of 'spielen' (to play)?
exercises.explanation: Regular verb: ge- + spiel- + t = gespielt. Wir haben Fußball gespielt = We played football.

exercises.hint: Vowel changes: i→u, add -en at end
exercises.explanation: 'trinken' is irregular: getrunken. The vowel changes (i→u) and -en is added: ge + trunk + en.

options: 'Be-' is an inseparable prefix, so no 'ge-' is added
exercises.explanation: Verbs with inseparable prefixes (be-, ge-, er-, ver-, ent-, zer-) do not add 'ge-' in the past participle: besuchen → besucht.

exercises.prompt: Match each infinitive to its Partizip II:

exercises.hint: Completely irregular — the stem changes entirely
exercises.explanation: 'gehen' is irregular: gegangen. Er ist gegangen = He went/has gone.

exercises.prompt: What is the Partizip II of 'arbeiten' (to work)?
exercises.explanation: Stems ending in -t add -et for pronunciation: ge + arbeit + et = gearbeitet.

sections.title: Haben or Sein?
sections.explanation: Most verbs use 'haben' as the auxiliary in Perfekt. However, some verbs use 'sein'. The rules for using 'sein': (1) Verbs of motion that imply a change of place: gehen (gegangen), fahren (gefahren), kommen (gekommen), fliegen (geflogen). (2) Verbs of state change: aufwachen (aufgewacht — to wake up), einschlafen (eingeschlafen — to fall asleep), sterben (gestorben — to die), werden (geworden — to become). (3) The verbs 'sein' (to be) and 'bleiben' (to stay/remain). Common 'sein' verbs: sein, bleiben, gehen, kommen, fahren, fliegen, laufen, passieren, aufwachen, einschlafen.
sections.tip: Use 'sein' with: motion verbs (go, come, drive, fly) and state-change verbs (wake up, fall asleep, become). Everything else uses 'haben'.

examples.de: Er ist mit dem Zug gefahren.
examples.en: He traveled by train. (fahren = motion → sein)
She woke up. (aufwachen = state change → sein)
I ate. (essen = no change of place → haben)

exercises.prompt: Which auxiliary does 'gehen' (to go) use in the Perfekt?
exercises.explanation: 'gehen' is a verb of motion (change of place) → uses 'sein': Ich bin gegangen.
'essen' has no motion or state change → uses 'haben': Ich habe gegessen.

exercises.prompt: Er ___ nach Berlin geflogen. (He flew to Berlin.) — fliegen = motion verb
exercises.explanation: 'fliegen' (to fly) implies a change of place → uses 'sein': Er ist nach Berlin geflogen.

exercises.prompt: Choose the correct Perfekt: 'She slept.' (schlafen — not a change of place)
exercises.explanation: 'schlafen' (to sleep, not the same as 'einschlafen' = to fall asleep) uses 'haben': Sie hat geschlafen.

exercises.prompt: Match each verb to its correct auxiliary in Perfekt:
Which verb uses 'sein' in Perfekt because of a state change?
exercises.explanation: 'aufwachen' (to wake up) involves a change of state (sleeping→awake) → uses 'sein': Ich bin aufgewacht.

exercises.prompt: Wir ___ in der Bibliothek geblieben. (We stayed in the library.) — bleiben uses sein
exercises.hint: 'bleiben' is one of the special 'sein' verbs
exercises.explanation: 'bleiben' (to stay) always uses 'sein': Wir sind geblieben.
